perturbation.original_text: Company shall provide and maintain, at its own expense, commercial general liability insurance and advertising injury coverage, with limits of not less than One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00), and shall cause such policy to be endorsed to state that Duval is an additional named insured thereunder. A certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage shall be furnished to Duval within thirty (30) days of the full execution of this Agreement. Such insurance policy shall provide that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such policy or remove Duval as an additional named insured without prior written notice to Duval at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall provide and maintain, at its own expense, commercial general liability insurance and advertising injury coverage, with limits of not less than One Million Dollars ($1,000,000.00), and shall cause such policy to be endorsed to state that Duval is an additional named insured thereunder. A certificate of insurance evidencing such coverage shall be furnished to Duval within sixty (60) days of the full execution of this Agreement. Such insurance policy shall provide that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such policy or remove Duval as an additional named insured without prior written notice to Duval at least ten (10) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text extends the deadline for providing the insurance certificate to 60 days, potentially conflicting with typical business practice that could have stricter insurance requirements, and reduces the prior written notice for policy termination to 10 days, which doesn't allow enough time to find a replacement. Given that the contract is governed by Ohio law, a shorter notification period may violate standards of reasonable notice, as determined on a case-by-case basis by courts or by specific industry regulations related to insurance contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 3937.31 requires that an insurance company provide a notice of cancellation or non-renewal at least thirty days prior to the effective date of such cancellation or non-renewal. The modified contract language stipulates only ten days' notice, which is a violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 3937.31 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 3937.31 | Policy period or guaranteed renewable successive policy periods. Ohio Revised Code / Title 39 Insurance / Chapter 3937 Casualty Insurance; Motor Vehicle Insurance Effective: September 22, 2013 Latest Legislation: House Bill 278 - 129th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Every automobile insurance policy shall be issued for a period of not less than one year or guaranteed renewable for successive policy periods totaling not less than one year. Where renewal is mandatory, "cancellation," as used in sections 3937.30 to 3937.39 of the Revised Code, includes refusal to renew a policy with at least the coverages, included insureds, and policy limits provided at the end of the next preceding policy period. No insurer may cancel any automobile insurance policy except pursuant to the terms of the policy, and in accordance with sections 3937.30 to 3937.39 of the Revised Code, and for one or more of the following reasons: (1) Fraud, concealment, or misrepresentation by the insured to the insurer of any material fact in the procurement or renewal of the insurance or in the submission of claims thereunder; (2) Loss of driving privileges through suspension, revocation, or expiration of the driver's or commercial driver's license of the named insured or any member of the named insured's family covered as a driver; provided that the insurer shall continue the policy in effect but exclude by endorsement all coverage as to the person whose driver's license has been suspended, revoked, or has expired, if the person is other than the named insured or the principal operator; (3) Nonpayment of premium, which means failure of the named insured to
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the insurer's notice period for termination or modification of the insurance policy to 10 days, while the Law Explanation states that Ohio Revised Code § 3937.31 requires at least thirty days' notice. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 concerns automobile insurance policies, the Law Explanation indicates the thirty-day notice requirement applies more broadly. The change introduces an inconsistency with the legally required notice period, thus creating a legal contradiction. The 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition applies because the modified text sets a deadline that violates state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: In the event a dispute arises under this agreement which cannot be resolved, such dispute shall be submitted to arbitration and resolved by a single arbitrator (who shall be a lawyer not employed by or associated with either party to this agreement) in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association then in effect. All such arbitration shall take place at the office of the American Arbitration Association located in Cleveland, Ohio. Each party is entitled to depose one (1) fact witness and any expert witness retained by the other party, and to conduct such other discovery as the arbitrator deems appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event a dispute arises under this agreement which cannot be resolved, such dispute shall be submitted to arbitration and resolved by a single arbitrator (who shall be a lawyer not employed by or associated with either party to this agreement) in accordance with rules determined solely by the arbitrator. All such arbitration shall take place at a location determined by the arbitrator. Each party is entitled to present their case, but pre-hearing discovery is entirely at the arbitrator's discretion and may be limited or denied.
perturbation.explanation: By giving the arbitrator sole discretion over discovery and the location of the arbitration, the modified text potentially conflicts with established arbitration law and principles of fairness. Ohio law generally expects reasonable discovery and a fair hearing location. The original text's adherence to AAA rules provided a baseline of fairness which is removed in the modified version.
perturbation.law_explanation: 9 U.S. Code § 10 outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award, including instances where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy. By granting the arbitrator total discretion to limit or deny pre-hearing discovery, the modified agreement increases the risk that an arbitration award could be vacated because a party was denied a fair opportunity to present their case. The original text's reference to AAA rules provides a procedural baseline that is more consistent with the intent of this section of the U.S. Code.

Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration- (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon s
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts established arbitration principles by granting the arbitrator sole discretion over discovery and location, potentially leading to unfair proceedings. 9 U.S. Code § 10(a)(3) allows for the vacation of an arbitration award if the arbitrators are "guilty of misconduct in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy." By removing the baseline of fairness provided by the AAA rules and giving the arbitrator complete control over discovery, the modified agreement increases the risk that a party will be denied a fair opportunity to present their case, which could be grounds for vacating the award under the mentioned US Code. The original text's reference to AAA rules provided a procedural baseline that is more consistent with the intent of this section of the U.S. Code, as the arbitrator's discretion would have been limited.

perturbation.original_text: Past due payments hereunder shall bear interest at the rate of (a) one and one-half percent (1-1/2%) per month, or (b) the maximum interest rate permissible under law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due payments hereunder shall bear interest at the rate of five percent (5%) per month.
perturbation.explanation: The modification sets the interest rate for late payments at 5% per month. This is a high interest rate that may violate usury laws, depending on the specifics of Ohio law and the nature of the transaction. The original text limited the interest to the maximum permissible under law, this change creates a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01(A) limits the interest rate to 8% per annum. A 5% monthly interest rate equates to 60% per annum, which is far above the legally permitted rate. This is a direct violation of Ohio's usury laws.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1343.01 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1343.01 | Maximum rate of interest. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1343 Interest Effective: June 29, 1988 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 130 - 117th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually, except as authorized in division (B) of this section. (B) Any party may agree to pay a rate of interest in excess of the maximum rate provided in division (A) of this section when: (1) The original amount of the principal indebtedness stipulated in the bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing exceeds one hundred thousand dollars; (2) The payment is to a broker or dealer registered under the "Securities Exchange Act of 1934," 48 Stat. 881, 15 U.S.C. 78A, as amended, for carrying a debit balance in an account for a customer if such debit balance is payable on demand and secured by stocks, bonds or other securities; (3) The instrument evidences a loan secured by a mortgage or deed of trust on real estate where the loan has been approved, insured, guaranteed, purchased, or for which an offer or commitment to insure, guarantee, or purchase has been received, in whole or in part, by the federal government or any agency or instrumentality thereof, the federal national mortgage association, the federal home loan mortgage corporation, or the farmers home administration, all of which is authorized pursuant to the "National Housing Act,"
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original clause, which limited interest on past due payments to the lower of 1.5% per month or the maximum permissible by law, with a fixed 5% monthly rate. The Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01(A) limits interest rates to 8% per annum unless certain conditions are met, and the 5% monthly rate equates to 60% annually, far exceeding this limit. Therefore, the modification creates a clear violation of Ohio's usury laws, unless the exceptions in division (B) apply, which is not indicated in the provided text.
